skill.name: qa-check
skill.description: Run pre pull request quality checks for frontend and backend projects. The skill guides lint, test, build, and coverage checks, then reports pass or fail status.
skill.summary: Run lint, test, build, and coverage checks before opening a pull request.
skill.author: Crearize

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command-looking strings and two high-risk weak-crypto matches. Review found the weak-crypto and entropy alerts to be false positives from Markdown and Japanese text, while the command findings are real QA instructions that execute local Gradle, pnpm, directory, and permission commands. This is publishable with a warning because project build scripts can run arbitrary local code.

medium_findings.title: Local QA Commands Execute Project Tooling
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run Gradle and pnpm commands for checks, builds, tests, and coverage. This is expected for a QA skill, but Gradle and package scripts are controlled by the target repository and may execute arbitrary local code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced commands are explicit and semantically required by the skill. They are legitimate QA operations, but they execute local build and test scripts from the repository.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem and Permission Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill asks the assistant to inspect paths, change directories, redirect errors to /dev/null, and propose chmod on gradlew. These operations are limited and explainable, but they still touch local filesystem state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem operations are directly present and align with environment validation. No destructive file operations or hidden persistence behavior were found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Match Is Not Substantive
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptography at the description and reference documentation lines. Review found no cryptographic implementation, hash operation, encryption call, or credential handling in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown metadata and a documentation path. There is no code that performs weak cryptography.

low_findings.title: High Entropy Heuristic Is Explained by Text Content
low_findings.description: The entropy alert applies to the Markdown file as a whole. Review found readable Markdown instructions in Japanese and no encoded payload, binary content, or concealed executable block.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The full file is human-readable Markdown. The higher entropy is consistent with multilingual text and formatting, not obfuscation.

low_findings.title: Command Examples Are User-Facing Usage Text
low_findings.description: Some command findings are slash-command examples for invoking the skill with frontend, backend, or coverage options. These are documentation examples, not shell execution payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are inside the usage section and show skill invocation syntax. They do not run external programs by themselves.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Execution Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill explicitly directs execution of local QA commands, including Gradle and pnpm scripts. Users should run it only in trusted repositories or reviewed branches.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in the skill workflow and are central to its function. The risk depends on the trusted state of the target repository.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Filesystem Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes pwd, ls, cd, and /dev/null redirection for environment checks. These are standard checks but still interact with local paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem commands are explicit and benign in context. I found no evidence of secret search, data exfiltration, or destructive deletion.

content.value_statement: Pull requests can miss broken builds, failing tests, and low coverage. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured QA flow before review.
actual_capabilities: Guides backend validation with Gradle check and coverage verification.
Guides frontend lint, build, and test coverage commands for pnpm projects.
Separates backend-only, frontend-only, and combined QA targets.
Defines pass, fail, and warning criteria for pre-PR quality gates.
Produces a QA report with command results, coverage status, and next steps.
limitations: It is tailored to a specific project layout with backend and frontend folders.
It depends on local Gradle, pnpm, and project scripts being available.
It does not prove that tests cover all important behavior.
It should be used only after reviewing commands in untrusted repositories.

use_cases.title: Pre-PR Validation
use_cases.description: Run the full QA checklist before opening a pull request.

use_cases.title: Frontend Release Check
use_cases.description: Check lint, build, tests, and UI coverage before merging frontend work.

use_cases.title: Backend Quality Gate
use_cases.description: Validate Gradle checks, static analysis, tests, and service coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Run All Checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use qa-check to run the standard backend and frontend QA flow. Report pass or fail with the main failure reasons.

prompt_templates.title: Check Frontend Only
prompt_templates.prompt: Use qa-check for the frontend target only. Include lint, build, test coverage, and any recommended fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Check Backend Without Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Use qa-check for the backend target and skip coverage checks. Summarize any Gradle, Checkstyle, SpotBugs, or test failures.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate QA Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use qa-check to rerun the failing area, identify the earliest failing command, explain likely causes, and propose a fix order.

output_examples.input: Run full QA before I create a pull request.
output_examples.output: A QA report lists backend and frontend results, coverage status, overall pass or fail, and the next fixes to complete.

output_examples.input: Check only the frontend and tell me what blocks merge.
output_examples.output: A focused report shows lint, build, and test coverage outcomes, then names the frontend issues that must be fixed.

output_examples.input: Run backend checks without coverage for a quick verification.
output_examples.output: A backend report shows Gradle check status, static analysis results, test outcomes, and whether the branch is ready for deeper QA.

best_practices: Review project scripts before running the skill on external branches.
Run the full QA flow before opening or updating a pull request.
Keep coverage checks enabled when validating merge readiness.
anti_patterns: Do not use it as the only security review for a pull request.
Do not skip coverage checks when changing business logic.
Do not run project scripts from an untrusted repository without inspection.

faq.question: What does this skill check?
faq.answer: It checks lint, tests, builds, and coverage for backend and frontend project areas.

faq.question: Can it run only frontend checks?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents a frontend target for lint, build, and coverage validation.

faq.question: Can it run only backend checks?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents a backend target for Gradle checks and coverage verification.

faq.question: Does it fix failures automatically?
faq.answer: No. It reports failures and suggests fixes, but remediation requires separate user approval or follow-up work.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill runs local build and test commands. Those commands are useful, but project scripts can execute arbitrary code.

faq.question: Which assistants can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
